chat.author: David Lawrence
chat.text: Some gaffers tape would fix the problem tho

chat.author: Ondřej Surý
chat.text: The right door (from my viewpoint) is still not properly closed.

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text:
@viktor The draft says  " DNS integrations should also be aware that global DNS domain names are not limited to ASCII characters, e.g., as described in [RFC5890].
...
Failure to account for such domain names may lead to inadvertent exclusion which could also lead to registrant and user confusion."
Is this saying that applications should work exclusively in IDNA u-label mode?  Or that they should operate in 8-bit clean DNS protocol (e.g. accepting names whose labels are comprised entirely of NUL bytes)?  Those are almost opposites, and I think the draft is not exactly advising either of those things?

chat.author: Ondřej Surý
chat.text: Thank you

chat.author: Viktor Dukhovni
chat.text: For most applications, only LDH labels and U-labels are the domain names of interest, and other, more specialised names (those e.g. ASCII names that violate RFC1123) are not in scope.

chat.author: Viktor Dukhovni
chat.text: The overlap is of course A-labels, and whether to support those directly or not is application-domain-dependent.

chat.author: Viktor Dukhovni
chat.text: Then sure some applications need to be able to process arbitrary 8-bit labels, that are not U-labels, but not ASCII, or ASCII, but not LDH, they may be out of scope, as e.g. not valid email domains, ...

chat.author: Michael StJohns
chat.text: @David - more than other rooms, these doors seem to actively resist being closed.

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text: I thin the advice is "Limit wire format DNS to LDH labels, except for underscore prefix labels, and prefer u-label for names presented to non-technical users".

chat.author: Viktor Dukhovni
chat.text: Do non-technical users need to be exposed to attrleaf labels? Or are you refering to ad hoc underscores in violation of LDH?

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text: I'm not suggesting that ordinary users should ever see Attrleaf labels.  I'm just noting that they are part of good practice in the wire format despite violating "Preferred Name Syntax".

chat.author: Viktor Dukhovni
chat.text: OK, perhaps we're largely in agreement and I merely did not get the right context from your comment at the mic.  The key other issue is whether IDNA means IDNA2008 or UTS#46 (which  is IMHO much too liberal in what it admits).

chat.author: Donald Eastlake
chat.text: Re SIG(0), note the existence of draft-eastlake-dnssd-rfc2931bis, which I presented in DNSSD earlier today.

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: I was just going to ask the status of the SIG(0) bis draft!

chat.author: Donald Eastlake
chat.text: The SIG(0) bis draft includes keystate.

chat.author: Maarten Wullink
chat.text: The RPP wg is also investigating how to enable secure delegation management by 3rd parties such as DNS operators, at the domain registration level

chat.author: Benno Overeinder
chat.text: Maarten, you can also comment on the mailing list as your question couldn't be asked in the room.  Thanks.

chat.author: Marco Davids
chat.text: Should the 'jabley@manta ~ % dig @9.9.9.9 NTAMUCH.ORG SOA +nocd +rec +multiline' in the draft return SERVFAIL to be more realistic ?

chat.author: Jim Reid
chat.text: Apart from DNS geeks, who will notice if an NTA was/wasn't involved?

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: @jim: no-one. But this doesn't seem to hurt anything....

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: it might also providfe some cover -- we are sometimes scared to implment NTAs for legal / liability reasons.

chat.author: Ondřej Surý
chat.text:
As implementor, I would be worried if the new EDE required some processing that could be used to slow down the resolver or blow up the DNS answers.
This is not the case and it was 3-liner for BIND 9

chat.author: Marco Davids
chat.text: Ignore my earlier suggestion, I go through all example scenario's.

chat.text: a lot of lines for the test

chat.author: Donald Eastlake
chat.text: Why not use .invalid (RFC 2606) rather than "."?

chat.author: Florian Obser
chat.text: :grinning_face_with_smiling_eyes:

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: "zone cut to nowhere" makes ACME DNS-01 challenges for internal hosts impossible. This is not a reason to stop this work, but the document should probably point out this problem.

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text: How are they possible otherwise?

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: I publish, on the Internet, _acme-challenge.host.internal.example.com TXT to obtain an LE cert to use on my internal host

chat.author: David Lawrence
chat.text: No ttl for refused either

chat.author: Florian Obser
chat.text: obviously it should be an AAAA record at the root

chat.author: David Lawrence
chat.text: .. so don't publish a delegation point if you don't want subdomains occluded?

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: David, yes. I'm just suggesting the document mentions this specific reason to not do the things in the document :)

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: Yah, what David and Peter said - we should note some advice for users.

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: I think the suggestion is to say that. :-)

chat.author: Peter van Dijk

chat.author: Warren Kumari

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: (earlier email from it was on 20250726 but reading it back it does not explain the problem well to somebody who .. did not already know)

chat.author: Ray Bellis
chat.text: not being able to query for an error type or put one in a zone is an argument for using a meta-type, not against.

chat.author: Peter Thomassen
chat.text: +1 Ray

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: The problem with EDNS is that it is hop-by-hop.

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: The beauty about EDNS is that it is hop-by-hop.

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: (sorry, but not entirely joking)

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: :smile:

chat.author: Jim Reid
chat.text: and forwarding isn't? :-)

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: Thinking about it, the resolver can return it's current TTL for a SERVFAIL or other response in replies. So EDNS can work.

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: (Thinking about SERVFAIL that it is propagating from another server, not SERVFAIL that it is generating.)

chat.author: Ray Bellis
chat.text: EDNS options are not transitive, despite that often being ignored

chat.author: Layal Jebran
chat.text: Agreed with Warren

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: EDNS is not transitive, except when it is. A bit like how the IETF does not make software APIs, except when it does.

chat.author: Ray Bellis
chat.text: there really ought to be a range of EDNS option codes reserved for true transitive use

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text: I'm most interested in using this for signaling DNS resolver overload to the stub.  That calls for resolver-wide scope, and binds the TTL to the EDE error code.

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: We have OPENPGPKEY and SMIME records already.  DKA enthusiasts might be interested in the HKP work, which contemplates a similar scheme to what you're describing.  HKP is an active working group document in the OpenPGP working group.  it uses SRV

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: my (quick) impression of this is "SRV+HKP+agreements on what 'not found' means", but with SRV and HKP replaced by new things

chat.author: Tobias Fiebig
chat.text: Yep.

chat.author: Donald Eastlake
chat.text: A separate CLASS can have a completely different set of root servers.

chat.author: avri doria

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: You can have any CLASS you want, as long as it is IN.

chat.author: Jim Reid
chat.text: Is the CHAOS class still a thing?

chat.author: Suzanne Woolf
chat.text: We looked at a separate CLASS for IDNs long ago, and alternate namespaces more recently. CLASS is way under-specified to use alongside INT.

chat.author: Pieter Lexis
chat.text: but there are so many other CLASS numbers :)

chat.author: Ondřej Surý
chat.text: Eugene Kashpureff can help with the new TLD!

chat.author: Suzanne Woolf
chat.text: Which is not to say it's still dubious, just that I'd want to see some of the previously identified challenges answered....

chat.author: Shane Kerr
chat.text: This is interesting. We don't need a new type necessarily... you can put an AAAA in the in-addr.arpa tree right?

chat.author: Ondřej Surý
chat.text: That's to say that IETF can't make ICANN to register anything.

chat.author: Suzanne Woolf
chat.text: Correct

chat.author: Peter van Dijk
chat.text: Shane, that doesn't give you prefix lengths, but we also have the APL type

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text: @shane Or follow the PTR and do a AAAA query there...

chat.author: Shane Kerr

chat.author: Suzanne Woolf
chat.text: @Ondrej there's been kind of a mutual understanding developed that nobody asks the other for permission but also they never tell each other what to do.

You can put whatever you want in the in-addr.arpa tree

chat.author: Suzanne Woolf
chat.text: Well, certainly claiming .utxo is a non-starter anywhere in the IETF. And alternate namespaces are no longer in the DNSOP charter.

chat.author: Ray Bellis
chat.text: @jim you said earlier that RRTYPEs do not require an I-D, but if the expert reviewers to not consider the specification  clear enough or stable enough we can insist upon it.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: I don't see how this helps with PQ at all.

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: i think his argument was that the 512-byte limit is problematic for large public keys

chat.author: Ondřej Surý
chat.text: We have TCP, we just need to learn how to use it properly

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: we also have DoQ and DoT, neither of which have those size limits

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text: I think the concern is about the 64K limit?

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text: (64k ought to be enough for anybody...)

chat.author: Ondřej Surý
chat.text: If we need 64K crypto, we should just drop DNSSEC.

chat.author: Ray Bellis
chat.text: this doesn't need an RRTYPE - it could be _da IN PTR.

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: ML-DSA-44 merely has 2420 byte signatures and 1312 byte public keys, so 64K would do.

chat.author: Benjamin Schwartz
chat.text: Dispatch to BoF.  This proposes a revolution in internet naming that is bigger than DNSOP, comparable to a DNS 2.0.

chat.author: Peter Thomassen
chat.text: @Ray just like it could be _deleg ;-p
